event: Congress enacts UIGEA (31 U.S.C. Sec. 5361-5367), criminalizing the acceptance of payments connected to unlawful internet gambling; it does not exempt games of skill and targets the payment rails.

'Black Friday': DOJ unseals United States v. Scheinberg, indicting PokerStars, Full Tilt and Absolute Poker (11 defendants) for UIGEA violations, bank fraud and money laundering; ~$3B forfeiture sought.
Government dismisses civil complaints against PokerStars and Full Tilt after settlement (no admission of wrongdoing); individual defendants face prison terms of ~3 months to 3 years.
NY AG Schneiderman issues cease-and-desist letters to DraftKings and FanDuel, calling DFS a scheme to evade gambling law and ordering them to stop accepting NY wagers.
Gov. Cuomo signs a law legalizing and regulating interactive/daily fantasy sports in New York as a game of skill (15% GGR tax; $50k registration) - the DFS gray-to-regulated playbook completes.
India's 28% GST on the full face value of online real-money gaming deposits takes effect, applied regardless of skill vs. chance - first major erosion of India's skill/chance line.

A federal grand jury (New Jersey federal prosecutors) is revealed to be probing AviaGames' covert bot use in Skillz's 2021-filed patent suit (N.D. Cal., No. 5:21-cv-02436, asserting U.S. Patents 9,649,564 and 9,479,602); Judge Freeman delays trial to Feb 2024.
Consumer class action Pandolfi v. AviaGames (No. 5:23-cv-05971) filed, alleging bots in supposed human-vs-human skill contests and asserting RICO claims against founders and investors.
N.D. Cal. jury finds AviaGames willfully infringed Skillz's patent and awards $42.9M; willfulness opens the door to treble damages.
Skillz sues rival Papaya Gaming (S.D.N.Y., before Judge Denise Cote) over the same bot-in-skill-games theory.
Skillz and AviaGames settle for around $80M ($50M cash plus royalties), ending the patent and unfair-competition litigation.
NY AG Letitia James stops 26 online sweepstakes casino platforms via cease-and-desist letters - the live gray-to-enforcement counterexample for workaround real-money models.
India's Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 receives assent, banning all 'online money games' - including games of skill - and criminalizing enabling/advertising/financing them.

S.D.N.Y. rules it 'undisputed' that Papaya Gaming used bots from 2019 to at least 2023 in Solitaire Cash, denying summary judgment - a second operator judicially found deploying non-genuine opponents.

Multi-state sweepstakes-casino crackdown crystallizes: California (AB 831, extending liability to vendors, signed Oct. 2025) and New York (SB 5935, signed Dec. 5, 2025) enact bans, joined by CT, MT and NJ; AZ/MI send 100+ C&D letters.
Papaya Gaming's $15M consumer class-action settlement (Barcelo v. Papaya) reaches its claim-filing deadline (final-approval hearing Mar 2026).

S.D.N.Y. jury awards Skillz $420M against Papaya Gaming for false advertising (reportedly the largest Lanham Act award in U.S. history) - misrepresenting bot opponents as real players now carries nine-figure exposure.

A divided Third Circuit holds the CFTC has exclusive jurisdiction over Kalshi's sports event contracts as 'swaps' preempting state gambling law - regulatory reclassification as a third path for workaround models (district courts remain split).
